scenario: Self-Managed cluster needs regular backups for disaster recovery. Team wonders backup approach.
question: Camunda 8 backup / restore strategy?

options.text: md:32-64. Path 1 -- Elasticsearch/OpenSearch: covers Zeebe + Operate + Tasklist + Optimize; all components backed up with the same backup ID (user-supplied integer, recommended Unix timestamp per :74-80); no downtime; continuous snapshots not supported. 8): covers Zeebe + Operate + Tasklist only (Optimize requires ES/OS path per :42-44); Zeebe and RDBMS backed up independently on their own schedules; Camunda aligns them at restore; backup IDs auto-generated by cluster (:82-84); supports point-in-time restore. Management API exposed via Spring Boot Actuator on port 9600 (Orchestration Cluster) or 8092 (Optimize) -- requires direct cluster access.

Camunda 8 Self-Managed does not support backup and restore -- operators must implement their own backup mechanisms from scratch -- this approach has limitations. 8). The backup management API is exposed via Spring Boot Actuator on the management port (9600 for Orchestration Cluster, 8092 for Optimize). Camunda provides detailed step-by-step procedures for both backup and restore operations, making backup capability a well-documented, first-class operational concern in Self-Managed deployments.

While the statement is technically true (SaaS backup is managed by Camunda), it is incomplete and misleading for the question context. The question asks about Self-Managed backup capabilities, which are substantial and documented. 8. Acknowledging SaaS handles it while ignoring the comprehensive Self-Managed backup documentation understates the operational capabilities available.

Camunda 8 Self-Managed backup is performed with a single CLI command that backs up all components atomically -- this approach has limitations. The backup procedure is multi-step and path-dependent. For ES/OS path: coordinate backup of Zeebe and all web applications using the same backup ID via separate API calls to each component's actuator endpoint, then trigger ES/OS snapshot. For RDBMS path: schedule Zeebe backups and RDBMS backups independently on their own schedules. There is no single unified backup command that atomically backs up all components simultaneously. md with separate sections for each path and component.

a.text: Correct. Camunda 8 backup strategy: Components to back up: Zeebe state: Journal + state files: on broker disks. Snapshots: Zeebe creates periodically. Backup mechanism: file-system snapshot (consistent state) or specific Camunda backup APIs. Operate / Tasklist data (Elasticsearch): ES snapshots: standard ES API. Configure repository: S3 / NFS / etc. Periodic snapshots: scheduled. Identity / Keycloak: Database backup: PostgreSQL / MySQL etc. Configuration backup: realms, clients, users. Configuration: Helm charts / yaml: source-controlled. Secrets: in secret manager (Vault) -- also backed up. Backup approaches: Approach 1: File-system snapshots: Use case: full cluster snapshot. Need: quiesce or atomic snapshot. Pros: simple. Cons: may need cluster pause. Approach 2: Per-component backup: Each component has its own backup method. Zeebe: dedicated mechanism. ES: snapshot API. DBs: standard tools. Approach 3: Coordinated backup: Use Camunda-provided tooling (verify availability). Orchestrates per-component backups. Backup schedule: Frequency: Production: daily typical. Critical data: more often. Retention: 7-30 days typical; longer per compliance. Backup storage: Off-cluster: don't store on same hardware. Cloud storage (S3, Azure Blob): durable. Encrypt: at rest. Access control: limit. Restore process: Steps: 1. Provision new cluster (or restore in-place). 2. Restore Zeebe state. 3. Restore ES indices. 4. Restore Identity / DB. 5. Verify: smoke test critical paths. Coordination: Components must be consistent: all from same point in time. Otherwise: process state may mismatch Operate data. Restore testing: Drill regularly: validate restore works. Document time-to-restore: RTO (Recovery Time Objective). Test edge cases: partial failures, etc. For SaaS: Camunda handles backups: managed. Restore on request: customer support process. Less customer responsibility. For DR (Disaster Recovery): RPO (Recovery Point Objective): how much data loss acceptable. 5 min: continuous replication. 1 hour: hourly backups. 1 day: daily backups. RTO (Recovery Time Objective): how fast restore. Minutes: hot standby. Hours: cold backup restore. Cross-region replication: For DR: replicate to different region. Camunda 8 features: verify per version. Process state migration: In-flight instances: may not survive across major recovery. Backup before major changes: rollback option. Best practices: Test restores regularly: backups untested = no backup. Document procedures: clear runbook. Automate: don't rely on manual. Monitor backup success: alert on failures. Encrypt backups: security. Off-site storage: protect against site disasters. For the scenario (regular backups): Daily backups of all components. Coordinated: same point-in-time. Off-site storage. Periodic restore drills. Anti-patterns: Backups never tested: discover broken at worst time. Single backup copy: lose if storage fails. Backups on same hardware: lose with system. No
